claim.query: volunteer gaelic patriotic movement duty nation labour protestant unionist minority

claim.thesis: Track whether plurality and civic language are displaced by a more disciplined nationalist editorial vocabulary.

supporting_documents.chunk_text:
became a leader-writer for provincial journals, Irish and Scottish, anq

a contributor to the reviews and magazines. Returning to Ireland ip

1849 he established in Belfast the ** Christian Patriot >’ (Is anything

now known of this journal?), and on its failure, he proceeded to Derry to

take up the editorship of the ‘‘ Standard,’” published in that city. In

the troublous times of 1850-54, he was one of the leaders in the League

of North and South, established by Duffy. Subsequently he was editor

of the Dublin ‘¢ Daily Express,’”’ for ten years, and Irish correspondent

of the ‘ Times’’ for seven yvears. His latest and finest journalistic

enterprise was the establishment of ‘‘ The National Review ”’ in Dublin

on 1lst June, 1868. This was commenced as a monthly of 24 pages,

contradictory_documents.chunk_text:
Boarding School for little boys under 12 years.

September 8th. For Prospectus apply to

THE SUPERIORESS.

Re-opens

A.M.D.G.

MUNGRET COLLEGE, S.J., LIMERICK.

Schools re-open Friday, September 1st.

This College, conducted by the Jesuit Fathers, com-

prises:—

% I.—PREPARATORY SCHOOL.

For Boys about the age of 11.

II.—BIGHER SCHOOL.

For Boys p-eparing for Professional, Civil Service,

Commercial, and Matriculation Examinations.

III.—AGRICULTURAL SCHOOL.

For Boys wishing to study Agriculture.

For terms, etc., apply to Rector.

THE BROTHERS OF THE CHRISTIAN SCHOOLS

The Brothers of the Christian Schools have now some vacancies.

in their Senior and Junior Novitiates at CASTLETOWN,

Queén’s Co., on account of the Annual Promotions just made.

papers. They also appeared in ‘‘ The Christian Brothers’ Magazine ™
(1907), with the heading * Lmes written by William Smith O’Brien—

The Irish Book Lover, vol. III, no. 5, December 1911
contextual_chunks.text:
THE IRISH BOOK LOVER 87

BOOK AUCTIONS.

A bound copy of the first volume of the ‘‘ Irish Book Lover’ was
auctioned at the salerooms of Messrs. James Adams and Sons, Dublin,
in the middle of November and knocked down at 9s. 6d. A very high
figure, especially for a periodical so recently established. This is the
frst instance of the ‘‘ I.B.I..”” coming under the hammer,

Dublin. | R. S. M.

Messrs. Bennett and Son, Upr. Ormond Quay, Dublin, sold a large
collection on 7th November. The titles and prices realised of some of

the works were as follows:—Trial of T. C. Luby, 1s. 6d.; Irish Penny
Vagazine, 1833, 1s. €a.; Petrie’s Ecclesiastical Architecture, 1845, 14s. ;
Barringtou’s Historic Memoirs, 2 vols., 5s.; The Tribune, a Dublin
Weekly, Nos. 1-14, 1855-56, 5s. 6d.; The Irish People, Nos. 1-43, 1863-
85, 6s. 6d.; Madden’s United Irishmen, 1lst ed., 7 vols., 34s.; second
'ed., 4 vols., £2 6s.; The Grand Juries of the County of Westmeath,
from 1727 till 1853, with Historical Appendix, compiled, pfinted and
bound by J. C. Lyons, Ledestown,; 1853, only 25 copies printed, rare, -
£3 5s.; Catalogue of Lough Fea Library, privately printed, 1872, 25s. ;
De Burgo’s H
